figures.gloss: The novelist, doctor, and national hero (1861–1896), shot by Spanish firing squad at Bagumbayan — and, in the Rizalista churches, far more: variously the Filipino Christ, a divine incarnation for the Malay race, or a deathless king who will return; his portrait stands on their altars and his December 30 martyrdom orders their calendar.
figures.facet: A divinization the historical man would have refused: the secular ilustrado becomes a god precisely through the pasyon grammar of innocent suffering — the people's Christ-template mattered more than Rizal's own rationalism.
figures.provenance: Reynaldo Ileto, 'Pasyon and Revolution: Popular Movements in the Philippines, 1840-1910' (1979); Prospero Covar's fieldwork on the Iglesia Watawat ng Lahi (1970s); Consolacion Alaras, 'Pamathalaan' (1988).

figures.gloss: The Tayabas lay brother (1815–1841) whose Cofradía de San José — founded in 1832 and barred from church recognition because it admitted only natives — gathered thousands at the foot of the Banahaw region until the colonial military crushed it in 1841 and executed him, displaying his severed head as a warning.
figures.facet: The proof that the Banahaw current predates Rizal: Pule's confraternity established the pattern — indigenous-led devotion, sacred mountain refuge, colonial martyrdom — into which Rizal's figure would later be received.
figures.provenance: Ileto (1979), whose second chapter reconstructs the Cofradía from trial records and surviving prayers.

figures.gloss: The volcano on the Quezon-Laguna border held by its devotees to be the new holy land — its springs, caves, and rock formations renamed as puwesto (sacred stations) with Calvary, Jordan, and Jerusalem transposed onto Tagalog terrain; the Rizalista and allied churches cluster at its foot in Dolores, and Holy Week fills its trails with pilgrims.
figures.facet: Sacred geography as repatriation: the Holy Land is not visited but declared to be HERE — the mountain answers colonial Christianity by relocating its entire map onto Filipino ground.
figures.provenance: Covar's Banahaw studies; Vicente Marasigan, 'A Banahaw Guru' (1985); Alaras (1988).

figures.gloss: The practice of visiting Banahaw's puwesto in sequence — bathing in the sacred springs, praying at the caves and rocks, and passing bodily ordeals like the Husgado cave, whose narrow passage is said to judge the pilgrim: the sinful stick fast, the cleansed crawl through.
figures.facet: Terrain as liturgy: the mountain itself administers the sacraments — water purifies, stone judges — with no priest required, which is precisely what made the practice ungovernable by the official church.
figures.provenance: Covar and Marasigan on puwesto practice; the Husgado ordeal is standard in the Banahaw ethnographic literature.

figures.gloss: The interpretive engine of the movements: the Tagalog pasyon — the chanted Passion epic of folk Catholicism — supplied the masses with a template of innocent suffering, death, and liberation through which national history is read, so that Rizal's martyrdom slots into Christ's place and the nation's redemption into the resurrection.
figures.facet: Ileto's thesis made visible: colonial catechesis handed the colonized the very grammar of their resistance — the pasyon taught peasants to recognize a redeemer, and they recognized Rizal.

figures.gloss: 'Church of the Banner of the Race,' the best-documented organized Rizalista denomination, in which Rizal is venerated as a divine figure and national redemption is read through his martyrdom; the church studied directly in Prospero Covar's fieldwork.
figures.facet: The flagship body that turns diffuse Rizal-veneration into an actual institution: a congregation, a liturgy, and a clergy built around a national hero rather than a traditional god.
figures.provenance: Prospero Covar's fieldwork on the Iglesia Watawat ng Lahi (1970s), already the anchor source for this shard's Rizal entry.

figures.gloss: A Banahaw-based church led by a female line of Supremas, holding the mountain as the new holy land and awaiting a Filipino-centered redemption; a standard subject of the Banahaw ethnographies and the clearest instance of the mountain complex's female religious leadership.
figures.facet: A counterweight to the roster's otherwise all-male founder pattern: the same sacred-mountain logic as the Rizalista churches, but its authority descends through a line of women.

figures.gloss: The legendary strongman pinned between the mountains of Montalban (Rizal province), whose struggles are said to cause earthquakes and who will one day break free to deliver the people — a pre-Hispanic-inflected folk-messianic figure documented well before Rizal's death.
figures.facet: The template, not the payoff: Ileto shows popular tradition transferring the Montalban legend's expectation of a returning liberator directly onto Rizal, making Carpio the folk-messianic frame Rizal's promised return was received into.
figures.provenance: Reynaldo Ileto, 'Pasyon and Revolution: Popular Movements in the Philippines, 1840-1910' (1979), already this shard's anchor source.

figures.gloss: The revealed divine name — parsed as God (Jove), King (Rex), of All (Al) — under which several Rizalista churches address Rizal's godhead directly, documented in the classic surveys of the Rizalista cults.
figures.facet: The clearest evidence that the movement passes well beyond hero-veneration: a specific revealed name of address is precisely how a tradition constitutes something as a god rather than merely honoring it.
figures.provenance: Marcelino A. Foronda Jr., 'Cults Honoring Rizal' (1961), the classic survey of the Rizalista churches.

figures.gloss: The 'Freedom Party' of Valentin de los Santos, a millenarian brotherhood relying on anting-anting (protective amulets) whose march on Manila on 21 May 1967 was met with police and military gunfire, killing dozens of members who believed themselves bulletproof.
figures.facet: Ileto's own epilogue case: the pasyon-messianic current stayed politically live into the late twentieth century, and its final collision with the modern state was fatal rather than merely suppressive, as with the nineteenth-century martyrdoms.
figures.provenance: Ileto, 'Pasyon and Revolution' (1979), epilogue; contemporary Philippine press accounts of the 21 May 1967 Manila clash.

edges.note: Ileto traces the line directly: the Cofradía's martyred brotherhood prepared the Banahaw pattern — mountain refuge, native-led devotion, colonial martyrdom — that received Rizal as its Christ.
GOLD SEAM: the Rizalista churches explicitly identify Rizal with Christ — a Tagalog Christ whose December 30 martyrdom re-enacts the Passion on Philippine soil.
GOLD SEAM: folk canonization without Rome — Rizal enters the intercessory economy of the saints, formalized as early as 1903 when the schismatic Philippine Independent Church canonized him.
GOLD SEAM (substrate): the puwesto continue the anito logic of springs, caves, and rocks as dwelling-places of power — pre-colonial sacred geography persisting under folk-Catholic names.
Divinized figures of the colonized who are awaited back — Rizal the returning king and John Frum the returning provider, each holding the promise that the colonial order will be overturned.
The flagship Rizalista church, in which Rizal is venerated as divine and studied directly by this shard's own anchor fieldwork.
One of the Banahaw-based churches clustered at the mountain's foot, holding it as the new holy land under a female line of Supremas.
Ileto documents popular tradition transferring the Montalban legend's expectation of a returning strongman onto the martyred Rizal.
The Carpio legend is the folk-messianic template the pasyon lens reads Rizal's own martyrdom and promised return through.
The revealed divine title under which some Rizalista churches address Rizal's godhead directly.

Ileto's own epilogue case: de los Santos's 1967 anting-anting brotherhood is the modern collision of the pasyon-messianic current with the state, read through the same interpretive engine as Rizal's own martyrdom.

dossier.essence: The Rizalista movements are a family of small Philippine folk-religious churches — the Iglesia Watawat ng Lahi and dozens of kin bodies, many clustered at the foot of Mount Banahaw — that venerate José Rizal, the novelist and national hero executed by Spanish firing squad on 30 December 1896, as far more than a hero: variously saint, divine incarnation, Tagalog Christ, or a king who did not truly die and will return. Memberships are dispersed and poorly enumerated; the current is a living stream of Philippine popular religion rather than a single organization.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmos is folk-Catholic — God, Christ, the Virgin, the saints — re-mapped onto Filipino ground and history: Mount Banahaw's springs, caves, and rocks are the puwesto, sacred stations where Calvary, Jordan, and Jerusalem are transposed onto Tagalog terrain, and the pasyon's template of innocent suffering and redemption organizes national history, with Rizal's martyrdom slotted into Christ's place. Many groups await Rizal's return and, with it, the nation's redemption.
dossier.practice: Practice centers on pilgrimage and commemoration: pamumuwesto, the sequential visiting of Banahaw's stations — bathing in sacred springs, praying in caves, passing bodily ordeals like the Husgado passage said to judge the pilgrim — alongside Holy Week observances, healing sessions, and rites each 30 December, the anniversary of Rizal's execution, before altars bearing his portrait. Individual churches maintain their own leadership lines, prayer forms, and hymnodies.
dossier.canon: No single canon: the chanted Tagalog pasyon (the Pasyong Mahal), Rizal's own writings — the novels and 'Mi último adiós' — read devotionally, and each church's received prayers, hymns, and revelations form an open, largely oral corpus.
dossier.reflects: Divinization caught in the act — a secular, rationalist martyr absorbed within decades into an inherited Christ-template, showing that the receiving grammar can matter more than the man.
sources: Reynaldo Ileto, 'Pasyon and Revolution: Popular Movements in the Philippines, 1840-1910' (1979)
Marcelino A. Foronda Jr., 'Cults Honoring Rizal' (1961)
Vicente Marasigan, 'A Banahaw Guru: Symbolic Deeds of Agapito Illustrisimo' (1985)
Prospero R. Covar, 'Larangan: Seminal Essays on Philippine Culture' (1998)
